summary: Charity Majors tears apart the concept of centralized 'command center' war rooms for monitoring applications, arguing they are a waste of money rooted in outdated thinking. She identifies two fatal flaws: graphs can be automated and only catch known problems, and the people watching graphs aren't the ones who can actually fix issues. The approach actively harms engineering feedback loops by insulating developers from the consequences of their code.
key_points: Centralized monitoring command centers are an outdated, wasteful approach to operations
Monitoring dashboards only catch known unknowns while most real problems are unknown unknowns
Only the engineers who build and maintain systems can effectively diagnose and fix them
Adding a human monitoring layer insulates developers from feedback about their code's behavior
Bugs are exponentially harder to fix the longer they go undetected
money_quotes: That extra human layer is worse than useless; it is actively harmful. By insulating developers from the consequences of their actions, you are concealing from them the information they need to understand the consequences of their actions.
The only people who can swiftly diagnose and fix complex systems issues are the people who build and maintain those systems, and those people are busy building and maintaining, not watching graphs.

The best time to find a bug is as soon as possible after writing it, while it's all fresh in your head. If you let it fester for days, weeks, or months, it will be exponentially more challenging to find and solve.

This path leads into a death spiral where alarms are going off constantly (yet somehow never actually catching the real problems), people getting burned out, and anyone competent will either a) leave or b) refuse to be on call.
key_insight: Centralized war rooms for monitoring are actively harmful because they break the essential feedback loop between developers and the production behavior of their code, and no amount of dashboard-watching can replace the contextual knowledge of the engineers who built the systems.
